brief:
Website Concept: AETHER

Industry
Ultra-luxury private aviation.

Goal
Sell a feeling of absolute exclusivity—not jets.

Brand

AETHER

"Time belongs to you."

No discounts.
No pricing.
No booking widget above the fold.

Everything should feel impossible to access.

Style
Apple cinematic quality
Swiss typography
Aman Resorts elegance
Rolls Royce restraint
Black + warm ivory
Gold used only for tiny accents
Huge whitespace
Almost editorial
Hero

Fullscreen.

Background:

A private jet slowly emerging from clouds at sunrise.

Very subtle.

Navigation almost invisible.

Center:

AETHER

Time belongs to you.

Small button

Request Membership

As you scroll...

The clouds slowly separate.

The jet subtly changes perspective using parallax.

Section 2

Instead of features...

Large editorial text.

Luxury isn't measured in leather seats.

It's measured in never waiting.

Huge typography.

Tiny image.

Section 3

Three gigantic cards.

Global Fleet

Minimal jet photography.

Personal Concierge

Not customer support.

One dedicated human.

Invisible Logistics

Transportation.

Hotels.

Security.

Handled before you think about them.

Cards animate upward while scrolling.

Section 4

Massive world map.

Thin glowing flight paths.

When hovering cities:

Tokyo

Dubai

Geneva

Monaco

Singapore

Los Angeles

Each city gently pulses.

Section 5

Numbers.

But presented like art.

97%

Flights prepared within 4 hours.

52

Countries served.

18 min

Average departure preparation.

Each number counts up.

Section 6

Aircraft gallery.

Very large horizontal scrolling section.

Each aircraft occupies almost the entire screen.

Minimal labels.

Nothing else.

Section 7

Editorial quote.

Every minute you wait is a minute someone else owns.

Huge serif typography.

Section 8

Membership process.

Three vertical steps.

01

Application

↓

02

Review

↓

03

Invitation

Connected by animated gold line.

Section 9

Testimonials

Not cards.

Magazine layout.

Portrait.

Large quote.

Tiny metadata.

Fade between testimonials automatically.

Section 10

Dark cinematic CTA.

Almost empty.

Only:

Your next departure isn't scheduled.

It's decided.

Button:

Request Membership

Motion Requirements

A2 should demonstrate knowledge of:

layered parallax
fade timing
stagger animations
scroll-triggered reveals
image masking
text splitting
counters
horizontal scroll
cursor interactions
hover depth
blur transitions
section-to-section pacing
viewport choreography
animation restraint
visual rhythm

Mezcal brand called Humo Santo, made in a single village (Santiago Matatlán, Oaxaca) by
one family's palenque (distillery), run by a third-generation maestro mezcalero named
Refugio. Every batch is wild-harvested Tobalá agave — takes 12-15 years to mature, grows
only on rocky hillsides, never cultivated, always foraged by hand. The brand's actual
point of view: most "artisanal" mezcal brands lie about their process for a nice label —
this one shows the ugly parts too (the roasting pit smoke, the mule that hauls the piñas
down the mountain, the copper stills that are older than the maestro himself). Positioning:
not a luxury spirits brand, a document of a dying craft — sold in unlabeled bottles with
a hand-written batch card, because Refugio doesn't believe in branding. Mood: earthy,
smoky, reverent, unpolished on purpose — the opposite of a clean modern spirits brand.

Clarifications: How should the page feel structurally as someone scrolls through it? → Vertical timeline; What should the site convince a visitor of first? → This is worth seeking out; Since Refugio 'doesn't believe in branding,' how prominent should the Humo Santo name/identity be? → Consistent throughout; What should ultimately happen when someone finishes reading? → Just witness the story. Use these as creative constraints; you still decide the sections and layout — the structure is not a fixed template.

Concept: VANTA Atelier

A bespoke mechanical watchmaker from Switzerland.
Every watch is made for a single client. Production is limited to 12 pieces per year.

Creative Direction

This is not ecommerce.

This is an experience.

Imagine if:

Apple designed the layout
Aesop designed the typography
Rolls Royce designed the luxury
Leica designed the photography
Rolex avoided every cliché on their website

The feeling should be:

silence
precision
confidence
obsession
craftsmanship

The website should almost feel like walking through an expensive museum.

Style

No gradients.

No glowing buttons.

No glassmorphism.

No neon.

Only

black
warm white
brushed steel
dark walnut
tiny champagne gold accents

Everything should breathe.

Massive whitespace.

Typography should do most of the work.

Hero

Full viewport.

Background isn't an image.

It's almost black.

In the center sits a perfectly lit mechanical watch floating in darkness.

Tiny dust particles slowly move.

Light subtly reflects across polished metal.

As you move your mouse, the reflections change slightly.

Above:

VANTA

Below

Time deserves more than mass production.

One elegant button

Reserve Consultation

No borders.

Simple underline hover.

Scroll

The watch slowly explodes into hundreds of components.

Every scroll reveals another internal layer.

Gears.

Bridges.

Springs.

Jewels.

Everything animated.

Not gimmicky.

Museum quality.

Section 2

Huge typography.

Only two words.

Built Slowly.

Underneath

A single paragraph explaining why every watch takes 1400 hours.

To the right

Macro photography filling the screen edge to edge.

Section 3

Horizontal scrolling timeline.

Year 0

Blank sketchbook.

↓

CAD drawings.

↓

Prototype.

↓

Hand polishing.

↓

Assembly.

↓

Testing.

↓

Final owner delivery.

Each card fills almost the whole viewport.

Section 4

Materials

Not cards.

Massive blocks.

Each material occupies an entire screen.

Brushed Titanium

Carbon Damascus

White Gold

Meteorite Dial

As each enters:

background changes

sound-wave inspired transition

macro texture fills screen

paragraph fades in

Section 5

Craftsmanship

Instead of statistics.

Use giant numbers.

1400

Hours.

200+

Hand Finished Components.

0

Assembly Line Workers.

12

Watches Per Year.

Huge typography.

Minimal decoration.

Section 6

Interactive movement

Large exploded 3D watch.

Hover any component.

It highlights.

Small label appears.

Balance Wheel

Escape Wheel

Bridge

Main Spring

Jewels

Rotating Crown

Everything beautifully animated.

Section 7

The Workshop

Cinematic.

Huge photographs.

Edge-to-edge.

Very little text.

Small captions.

Like an architecture magazine.

Section 8

Meet the Maker

Portrait.

Very understated.

Story written almost like a letter.

No corporate language.

Section 9

Reservation Process

A vertical journey.

01

Private conversation.

↓

02

Movement selection.

↓

03

Material choice.

↓

04

Hand finishing.

↓

05

Delivery one year later.

Each step animates into place.

Footer

Almost empty.

Only

VANTA

Swiss Atelier

12 Watches Per Year

Reserve Consultation

Tiny legal links.

Motion Language

Every animation should feel expensive.

buttery smooth 120fps feel
no bouncing
no exaggerated easing
opacity fades
slow parallax
image masking
typography reveals
text tracking animations
smooth horizontal transitions
cinematic page transitions
subtle lighting movement
scroll-linked storytelling

Nothing should feel like a website template.

Design Constraints
No boxed sections.
No obvious cards unless absolutely necessary.
Use asymmetrical layouts.
Alternate full-bleed imagery with whitespace.
Every section should have a unique composition.
Typography should create hierarchy rather than colored backgrounds.
Images should dominate where appropriate.
Buttons should be understated and elegant.
Every viewport should feel like a premium editorial spread.
Introduce visual surprises every few scrolls so the experience never becomes repetitive.
Build custom components instead of relying on common UI libraries.
Ensure every transition between sections feels intentional, almost like moving between scenes in a documentary.

Clarifications: The brief describes a very specific scroll experience (watch exploding into components, horizontal timelines, full-screen material blocks). How ambitious should the overall composition rhythm be? → Full cinematic scroll-scenes; Given this audience only reserves a consultation (no cart, no price shown), what should 'Reserve Consultation' feel like as the primary action? → Single quiet gateway; How heavily should the exploded/interactive 3D watch (Section 6) carry the storytelling versus photography and typography? → 3D as the centerpiece; Scroll-linked animation is central to the brief but can exclude reduced-motion/screen-reader users. How should we handle this tension? → Full motion, graceful fallback. Use these as creative constraints; you still decide the sections and layout — the structure is not a fixed template.

Boutique hotel called Marrow, a converted 1920s warehouse in Lisbon's Alfama district.
Twelve rooms, no two alike, exposed original brick and steel beams left intentionally
raw. Positioning: not a chain, not minimalist-Scandinavian-every-hotel-looks-like-this —
warm, textured, a little imperfect on purpose. Mood: editorial, warm, tactile, intimate —
think a design magazine feature, not a booking-site template.

i want you to build a dashboard likelovable you have thw first page where you input yor promot a sidebar wiht yur session then you click the promot and the you o to the next page modula,r first left halt chat the other shows either the code design whatever

Clarifications: What's the primary output your users will generate from prompts? → Mixed outputs; Who are your core users? → Everyone; What should the chat panel (left side) do? → Full AI assistant; What's the tone you want for the interface? → Professional & minimal. Use these as creative constraints; you still decide the sections and layout — the structure is not a fixed template.

Website Concept: AURELIS — Swiss Luxury Engineering Studio
Brand Overview

Create a premium website for Aurelis, a Swiss engineering and innovation studio that designs advanced mechanical systems, precision instruments, and architectural technology.

The company should feel like a combination of:

Swiss watchmaking precision
Apple product simplicity
Ferrari engineering emotion
a luxury architecture firm

The website must communicate:
precision, innovation, craftsmanship, exclusivity, and technological mastery.

Avoid generic startup aesthetics. No gradients everywhere, no glassmorphism, no template sections.

The feeling should be:

"This company builds the machines that define the future."

Visual Direction
Style
Minimal luxury
Swiss editorial design
High contrast
Extremely clean spacing
Architectural composition
Premium typography
Subtle motion

References:

Swiss International Typographic Style
Apple product pages
Porsche design language
high-end architecture portfolios
Colors

Primary:

Warm off-white background (#F5F3EE)
Deep charcoal (#111111)

Accent:

Brushed titanium grey
Small electric blue highlights

Avoid:

neon colors
excessive gradients
rounded cartoon UI
startup purple/blue SaaS style
Global Layout
Navbar

Fixed transparent navbar.

Left:
AURELIS logo

Center:

Engineering
Projects
Technology
Studio
Journal

Right:
"Contact Studio" button

Design:

thin typography
uppercase labels
subtle hover animations
no hamburger unless mobile
Hero Section

Full viewport.

Background:
A cinematic 3D render/video of a futuristic mechanical device slowly rotating.

Content:

Small label:
"SWISS PRECISION ENGINEERING"

Large headline:

"Engineering objects
for the next century."

Subheadline:

"Aurelis combines advanced materials, computational design, and human craftsmanship to create technology without compromise."

Buttons:

Primary:
"Explore Projects"

Secondary:
"Discover Our Process"

Hero Interaction

When scrolling:

image slowly moves upward
typography fades and scales
subtle parallax
smooth cinematic transition

No flashy animations.

Section 2 — Philosophy

Large editorial section.

Left:

Huge text:

"Precision is not a feature.
It is a discipline."

Right:

Short explanation:

"Every Aurelis creation begins with thousands of decisions. Material selection, structural optimization, manufacturing tolerances, and human interaction are refined until nothing unnecessary remains."

Add:

animated line drawings
technical sketches
blueprint effects
Section 3 — Featured Projects

Large portfolio showcase.

Three projects.

Project 01

Name:
Aurelis Chronos

Category:
Precision Instrument

Visual:
Luxury mechanical object

Information:
"An autonomous measurement system inspired by traditional watchmaking."

Interaction:
Hover reveals technical specifications.

Project 02

Name:
Aurelis Aero

Category:
Advanced Mobility

Visual:
Futuristic vehicle component

Project 03

Name:
Aurelis Habitat

Category:
Architectural Technology

Visual:
Minimal futuristic building

Layout:
Not normal cards.

Use:

giant images
asymmetric grids
editorial magazine layout
Section 4 — Technology

Dark section.

Title:

"The science behind simplicity."

Display 4 technologies:

Computational Design

AI-assisted engineering simulations.

Advanced Materials

Titanium, carbon composites, ceramic alloys.

Precision Manufacturing

Micron-level production.

Human Craft

Engineers and artisans working together.

Design:

Large numbers:

01
02
03
04

with animated transitions.

Section 5 — Process

Horizontal timeline.

Stages:

01
Research

02
Simulation

03
Prototype

04
Manufacturing

05
Final Assembly

Each stage expands on hover.

Section 6 — Studio

Luxury company introduction.

Large image:
Engineers working inside a clean Swiss workshop.

Text:

"Based in Switzerland, Aurelis brings together engineers, designers, and craftsmen united by one goal: creating objects that remain relevant decades from now."

Section 7 — Journal

Editorial articles.

Three articles:

"Why mechanical simplicity is harder than complexity"

"The future of computational manufacturing"

"Designing objects humans keep forever"

Layout:
Like a luxury magazine.

Final CTA

Full screen.

Black background.

Text:

"Build something timeless."

Button:

Start a conversation

Minimal footer.

Technical Requirements

The generated website should include:

Fully responsive design
Smooth scroll animations
Custom cursor
Page transitions
Image reveal animations
Hover micro-interactions
Magnetic buttons
Animated typography
Clean component architecture
No generic templates

Components:

premium navbar
hero animation system
portfolio gallery
editorial sections
timeline component
luxury footer

Clarifications: What level of detail should it have? → Interactive & animated; Who is the primary audience? → Professionals; What tone should it strike? → Calm & premium. Use these as creative constraints; you still decide the sections and layout — the structure is not a fixed template.

Website: Forge & Oak
Business

A premium custom furniture workshop.

They create handmade wooden furniture: tables, chairs, shelves, and interiors.

Goal

Sell high-end custom pieces and make visitors trust the craftsmanship.

Style
Premium but simple
Warm
Modern craftsmanship
Not luxury fashion
More "Apple meets a woodworking studio"
Colors
Off-white background
Dark charcoal text
Wood tones
Small green/brown accents
Layout
1. Hero

Large image of a handcrafted wooden table/workshop.

Text:

Furniture built for generations.

Small description:
"Handcrafted pieces designed, built, and finished in our studio."

Buttons:

View Collection
Start a Custom Project
2. Philosophy

Split section:

Left:
Large image of craftsman working.

Right:
"Our approach"

3 points:

Natural materials
Traditional techniques
Modern design
3. Featured Collection

3 cards:

Dining Tables
Lounge Furniture
Custom Interiors

Each with image + short description.

4. Process

Horizontal timeline:

01 Design consultation
↓
02 Material selection
↓
03 Hand construction
↓
04 Final installation

5. Featured Project

Large case study:

"Oak Residence"

Image gallery + short explanation.

6. Testimonials

3 customer quotes.

7. Contact

"Create your custom piece."

Simple form.

Clarifications: Who are your primary visitors likely to be? → Both equally; What's the single action you most want visitors to take? → Browse existing pieces; How much of the storytelling should focus on *you* (the maker) vs. *the process*? → Balanced blend; For the testimonials section, what matters most to showcase? → Both equally. Use these as creative constraints; you still decide the sections and layout — the structure is not a fixed template.

High-intensity fitness brand called Kinetic Forge — strength training and athletic
performance coaching for serious lifters, not casual gym-goers. Positioning: brutal
honesty over motivation-poster fluff, results measured in numbers not vibes. Mood:
aggressive, high-energy, kinetic, intense — think a boxing gym crossed with a data
dashboard, not a typical fitness influencer aesthetic.

Clarifications: What level of detail should it have? → Detailed & rich; Who is the primary audience? → Customers / users; What tone should it strike? → Warm & inviting. Use these as creative constraints; you still decide the sections and layout — the structure is not a fixed template.
a bold editorial site for an independent coffee roaster, warm and cinematic

umen Coffee

Business:
A premium specialty coffee roastery selling freshly roasted beans online and in one flagship café.

Audience:
Coffee enthusiasts who care about quality and origin.

Style:
Warm, earthy, modern, Scandinavian.

Content:

Hero
Our Story
Featured Coffees
How We Roast
Brewing Guides
Customer Reviews
Visit Our Café
Footer

Clarifications: Who is the primary audience you want to attract to the site? → Coffee Connoisseurs; What is the primary action you want visitors to take on the website? → Visit Café; Which tone and visual style best reflects your brand identity? → Warm Earthy Modern. Use these as creative constraints; you still decide the sections and layout — the structure is not a fixed template.

Peak Performance Clinic – Sports medicine and recovery center with treatments, doctors, testimonials, and booking.

Clarifications: What level of detail should it have? → Interactive & animated; Who is the primary audience? → General public; What tone should it strike? → Bold & energetic. Use these as creative constraints; you still decide the sections and layout — the structure is not a fixed template.

Website Idea: Veridian Capital

Industry: Private investment firm / Venture Capital

Company

Veridian Capital invests in companies building the future of manufacturing, AI, energy, robotics, and infrastructure.

Unlike most VC firms, they don't chase trends—they partner with founders for decades.

The brand should feel:

Intelligent
Trustworthy
Premium
Modern
Editorial
Calm

Think a mix of Stripe, Notion, and a luxury consulting firm, not crypto or fintech hype.

Goal

Convince exceptional founders to apply for funding.

Secondary goal:

Build trust with institutional investors.

Website Structure

This is content-heavy.

Not just six sections.

1. Hero

Large statement.

Short explanation.

Beautiful background.

Strong CTA.

2. Portfolio Highlights

Featured companies.

Metrics.

Industries.

Case studies.

3. Investment Philosophy

Long editorial section.

Multiple content blocks.

Illustrations.

Quotes.

Timelines.

4. Areas We Invest In

Cards for:

AI
Robotics
Climate
Healthcare
Infrastructure
Manufacturing
Defense
Enterprise Software
5. Featured Companies

Large grid.

Company logos.

Descriptions.

Funding stage.

6. Founder Stories

Rich testimonials.

Portraits.

Pull quotes.

Video links.

7. Research & Insights

Blog-style layout.

Featured articles.

Reports.

Whitepapers.

Market analysis.

8. Team

Large editorial team profiles.

Timeline.

Backgrounds.

Experience.

9. Investment Process

Step-by-step.

Animated timeline.

Icons.

Content.

10. FAQ

Detailed.

Expandable.

11. Careers

Open roles.

Culture.

Benefits.

Photos.

12. Contact

Beautiful contact section.

Map.

Offices.

Design Direction

Editorial.

Huge typography.

Excellent spacing.

Minimal but not empty.

Large photography.

Clean cards.

Subtle animations.

Soft gradients.

Beautiful charts.

Elegant icons.

Motion
Smooth reveals
Card elevation
Timeline animations
Number counters
Logo marquees
Hover transitions
Image zooms
Sticky navigation

Nothing excessive.

Why this is a great benchmark

It tests whether PD Studio can handle:

Large information architecture
Content hierarchy
Editorial layouts
Varied section designs (without every block looking the same)
Real-world business needs
Elegant interactions
Consistent visual language across a long page

This is the kind of project where a mediocre AI quickly becomes repetitive, while a strong design engine can keep introducing fresh compositions and maintain a coherent, premium feel from top to bottom.
a landing page for a ceramics studio
a supply chain mockup for a merch store

Swiss Watch Atelier ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

Brief

A family-owned Swiss watchmaker that hand-builds only 30 mechanical watches per year. Every watch is assembled by a single craftsman over several months. The website should feel like precision engineering, silence, patience, and timeless luxury rather than modern technology.

Challenges

Editorial storytelling
Cinematic product reveals
Luxury typography
Macro photography
Minimal UI
Slow animations

Clarifications: Who is the primary audience for the site? → Rich people; What is the main conversion goal? → "This is not a product. This is a masterpiece created through patience, precision, and human craftsmanship."; Which visual element should dominate the hero section? → Imagine landing on the page and seeing:  A master watchmaker's hands assembling a movement. A macro shot of intricate gears, screws, and jewels. Soft natural light highlighting brushed steel and polished edges. Shallow depth of field so only the essential details are in sharp focus.  The watch isn't just shown—it feels alive through the precision of its construction.  The headline becomes secondary, almost like a museum caption.; What tone should the copy convey? → Primary tone  Quiet confidence.  The brand never needs to tell people it's exceptional. It lets the work speak for itself.  Tone characteristics Calm  Short, deliberate sentences.  No urgency.  No hype.  Example:  Every watch begins with time, not speed.  Precise  Every word should feel intentional.  Avoid exaggerated language.  Instead of:  The world's most incredible luxury watch.  Use:  Finished by a single craftsman over several months.  Specific facts create credibility.  Humble  The brand respects the craft more than itself.  Example:  We do not compete with time.  We work alongside it.  Poetic, but restrained  There can be emotion, but it should never become overly dramatic.  Good:  Precision deserves patience.  Too much:  Every heartbeat echoes through eternity, transcending the infinite cosmos...  Intelligent  Assume the visitor appreciates quality.  Don't over-explain.  Leave room for discovery.  Timeless  Avoid trendy startup language.  Never use:  Revolutionary Game-changing Next-generation Disruptive Cutting-edge AI-powered World-class  Those phrases age quickly.  Emotional qualities  The copy should evoke:  Respect Curiosity Calm Patience Heritage Precision Exclusivity Quiet luxury  Not:  Excitement Urgency FOMO Aggressive selling Writing style  Think:  Short paragraphs Large amounts of whitespace Strong headlines Minimal body copy Every sentence earns its place  The page should feel more like reading a beautifully designed book than scrolling through an advertisement.  Brand voice examples  Instead of:  We build premium Swiss watches with exceptional quality.  Write:  Every movement is assembled by one pair of hands.  Instead of:  Our watches are made from the finest materials.  Write:  Steel, sapphire, and time. Nothing more than what is necessary.  Instead of:  We have over 50 years of experience.  Write:  Some techniques cannot be hurried. They can only be learned.  Overall inspiration  If you had to describe the voice in a few words, it would be:  Confident, never loud Elegant, never ornate Precise, never technical for its own sake Emotional, never sentimental Luxurious, never ostentatious  The reader should come away with the feeling that the brand has nothing to prove—and that's exactly why it's worth paying attention to.. Use these as creative constraints; you still decide the sections and layout — the structure is not a fixed template.

choices.q1: "This is not a product. This is a masterpiece created through patience, precision, and human craftsmanship."
choices.q2: Imagine landing on the page and seeing:  A master watchmaker's hands assembling a movement. A macro shot of intricate gears, screws, and jewels. Soft natural light highlighting brushed steel and polished edges. Shallow depth of field so only the essential details are in sharp focus.  The watch isn't just shown—it feels alive through the precision of its construction.  The headline becomes secondary, almost like a museum caption.

No urgency.  No hype.  Example:  Every watch begins with time, not speed.  Precise  Every word should feel intentional.  Avoid exaggerated language.  Instead of:  The world's most incredible luxury watch.  Use:  Finished by a single craftsman over several months.  Specific facts create credibility.  Humble  The brand respects the craft more than itself.  Example:  We do not compete with time.  We work alongside it.  Poetic, but restrained  There can be emotion, but it should never become overly dramatic.  Good:  Precision deserves patience.  Too much:  Every heartbeat echoes through eternity, transcending the infinite cosmos...  Intelligent  Assume the visitor appreciates quality.  Don't over-explain.  Leave room for discovery.  Timeless  Avoid trendy startup language.  Never use:  Revolutionary Game-changing Next-generation Disruptive Cutting-edge AI-powered World-class  Those phrases age quickly.  Emotional qualities  The copy should evoke:  Respect Curiosity Calm Patience Heritage Precision Exclusivity Quiet luxury  Not:  Excitement Urgency FOMO Aggressive selling Writing style  Think:  Short paragraphs Large amounts of whitespace Strong headlines Minimal body copy Every sentence earns its place  The page should feel more like reading a beautifully designed book than scrolling through an advertisement.  Brand voice examples  Instead of:  We build premium Swiss watches with exceptional quality.  Write:  Every movement is assembled by one pair of hands.  Instead of:  Our watches are made from the finest materials.  Write:  Steel, sapphire, and time. Nothing more than what is necessary.  Instead of:  We have over 50 years of experience.  Write:  Some techniques cannot be hurried. They can only be learned.  Overall inspiration  If you had to describe the voice in a few words, it would be:  Confident, never loud Elegant, never ornate Precise, never technical for its own sake Emotional, never sentimental Luxurious, never ostentatious  The reader should come away with the feeling that the brand has nothing to prove—and that's exactly why it's worth paying attention to.

brief:
A dashboard for a community garden

Creative direction: Cinematic editorial. Palette: Warm ivory & ember. Typography: Elegant serif display. Motion: Story on scroll. Structure: Let the agent decide. Surfaces: Landing page, Product page, About / story, Pricing, Waitlist. Treat these as creative constraints — you still decide the actual sections and layout; the structure is not a fixed template.

choices.surfaces: Landing page, Product page, About / story, Pricing, Waitlist

brief:
Experience Concept — Atelier Noir
A luxury Swiss coffee house where every machine is handcrafted and every cup is engineered to perfection.

Do not build a traditional landing page.

The website should feel like a cinematic journey through a Swiss workshop. Visitors should feel as if they are walking through an exclusive factory, an artisan studio, and finally into the café itself. Every scroll reveals another layer of craftsmanship. The goal is not to sell coffee—it is to communicate obsession with precision.

Overall Mood

The website should feel calm, slow, luxurious, and intentional.

Nothing should appear instantly.

Nothing should move quickly.

Every animation should feel heavy, mechanical, and expensive.

Imagine the pacing of a luxury watch commercial.

Opening Scene

The page loads into complete darkness.

Only faint ambient sounds.

A single spotlight slowly fades in.

As the light grows, an incredibly detailed custom espresso machine is revealed.

Polished stainless steel.

Brass fittings.

Walnut handles.

Soft reflections move naturally across the metal.

Steam slowly escapes from a pressure valve.

The machine is alive before any text appears.

The camera slowly pushes toward it.

Only after several seconds does elegant typography fade in.

Precision, brewed in Switzerland.

The user immediately understands this is not an ordinary coffee company.

Scroll Sequence 1 — Engineering

As scrolling begins, the camera slowly circles the machine.

Tiny mechanical movements begin.

Pressure gauges rotate.

Indicator lights softly illuminate.

Steam valves release tiny bursts of vapor.

Then the machine gently separates.

Not explosively.

Each handcrafted component slowly floats outward as if suspended in zero gravity.

The boiler.

Copper tubing.

Pressure chamber.

Steam system.

Portafilter.

Temperature sensors.

Every part rotates independently.

Hovering any component reveals an elegant annotation explaining its purpose and how it was individually manufactured.

The machine slowly reassembles before continuing.

Scroll Sequence 2 — The Workshop

The environment transforms seamlessly.

The machine fades into the actual Swiss workshop where it was built.

Large cinematic shots appear naturally through parallax movement.

Engineers polish stainless steel by hand.

Woodworkers carve walnut handles.

Brass components are machined on precision equipment.

Technicians calibrate pressure systems.

The camera never cuts abruptly.

It slowly glides through the workshop as though the visitor is physically walking inside.

Tiny particles float through warm workshop lighting.

Dust catches sunlight.

Everything feels authentic.

Scroll Sequence 3 — Materials

Instead of listing specifications, every material becomes an experience.

The camera moves across enormous macro shots.

Brushed steel.

Hand-polished brass.

Leather stitching.

Solid walnut grain.

The lighting changes as the mouse moves, revealing tiny imperfections and reflections.

Scrolling changes the viewing angle rather than jumping to another section.

Each material receives only one sentence.

Minimal.

Confident.

No marketing language.

Scroll Sequence 4 — Coffee Origins

The workshop slowly disappears.

The floor beneath the visitor transforms into a large interactive world map.

Elegant illuminated lines connect Switzerland with farms across the world.

As users move their cursor across the map, beans begin travelling toward Switzerland.

Every origin opens a cinematic scene.

Morning mist over Ethiopian mountains.

Brazilian farms at sunrise.

Colombian harvests.

Not cards.

Not grids.

Entire immersive scenes.

Every origin tells a story through movement.

Scroll Sequence 5 — Roasting

The atmosphere becomes darker.

The visitor enters the roasting room.

Beans slowly fall through the air in slow motion.

Heat waves distort the background.

The roasting drum rotates gently.

Smoke rises naturally.

As scrolling continues, bean colors gradually transform from green to perfectly roasted.

The aroma is suggested visually through delicate flowing particles and warm cinematic lighting.

The website feels almost tangible.

Scroll Sequence 6 — Extraction

The espresso machine returns.

Everything slows.

The camera moves extremely close.

Water begins flowing through the machine.

Pressure slowly increases.

The gauge needle rises.

Tiny vibrations appear.

The portafilter locks into position with a satisfying mechanical movement.

Then...

The very first drop of espresso falls.

Time almost freezes.

The crema slowly forms.

Steam rises naturally.

Soft reflections move across the liquid.

The camera watches without interruption.

No text appears.

The coffee itself becomes the storytelling.

Scroll Sequence 7 — The Café

The espresso slowly dissolves into the interior of the café.

Natural stone.

Dark oak.

Soft leather seating.

Warm architectural lighting.

Large windows looking over Zurich.

Customers speak quietly.

Baristas move with confidence.

Nothing feels rushed.

The camera walks through the café like a guest entering for the first time.

Every corner reveals another carefully designed detail.

Scroll Sequence 8 — Philosophy

The environment becomes almost empty.

Large white space.

One sentence slowly fades into view.

Perfection is never manufactured. It is crafted.

Nothing competes with the typography.

Silence becomes part of the experience.

Final Scene

The visitor arrives back at the original espresso machine.

Now they understand everything behind it.

The workshop.

The engineering.

The farmers.

The roasting.

The café.

The machine is no longer just a product.

It represents hundreds of hours of craftsmanship.

The camera slowly rotates around it one final time.

Steam rises gently.

Soft light reflects across the polished metal.

Only one elegant button appears beneath it.

Reserve Your Experience

No aggressive call to action.

No discounts.

No promotions.

The confidence comes from restraint.

Motion Philosophy

Every transition should feel like a camera move, not a page change. Scenes dissolve into one another through lighting, reflections, depth, and movement. Scrolling should control the pace of discovery rather than simply revealing stacked sections. Animations are slow, deliberate, and physically believable—steam curls naturally, polished metal catches shifting light, machinery moves with weight, and the camera glides as if filmed on a precision dolly. The website should feel closer to a luxury brand film than a conventional landing page.

The visitor should finish the experience with one lasting impression:

"These people don't make coffee. They perfect every detail behind it."

Creative direction: Cinematic editorial. Palette preference: Emerald prestige. Typography: Cormorant Garamond + Karla. Structural approach: Zigzag. Motion preference: Story on scroll. Product surfaces: Product page. Treat these as design constraints, not as a component template.

Experience Concept — Atelier Noir
A luxury Swiss coffee house where every machine is handcrafted and every cup is engineered to perfection.

Do not build a traditional landing page.

The website should feel like a cinematic journey through a Swiss workshop. Visitors should feel as if they are walking through an exclusive factory, an artisan studio, and finally into the café itself. Every scroll reveals another layer of craftsmanship. The goal is not to sell coffee—it is to communicate obsession with precision.

Overall Mood

The website should feel calm, slow, luxurious, and intentional.

Nothing should appear instantly.

Nothing should move quickly.

Every animation should feel heavy, mechanical, and expensive.

Imagine the pacing of a luxury watch commercial.

think we're missing a fundamental layer of design intelligence.

Right now the agent thinks in sections:

Hero
↓
Features
↓
More Features
↓
CTA

That's producing websites that feel mechanically assembled rather than intentionally designed.

I want the agent to think in design patterns and storytelling, not page sections.

Before deciding what components to use, it should first decide how the narrative unfolds and why each section exists.

For example:

Goal:
Build curiosity.

Pattern:
Reveal before Explain.

Structure:
Hero
↓
Interactive Product Preview
↓
Benefits
↓
Feature Details

Why:
People understand products faster when they see them before reading about them.

Another example:

Goal:
Maintain visual interest.

Pattern:
Visual Rhythm.

Rule:
Alternate large, small, wide, dense and whitespace-heavy layouts.

Why:
Repeating equal-sized cards creates visual fatigue.

Or:

Goal:
Increase trust progressively.

Pattern:
Escalation.

Structure:
Vision
↓
Product
↓
Proof
↓
Testimonials
↓
Pricing
↓
CTA

Why:
Each section answers the user's next objection.

I think we should introduce a new knowledge category, something like:

knowledge/
    layout_patterns/
    storytelling_patterns/
    hierarchy_patterns/
    visual_rhythm/
    ux_psychology/

Each entry should contain:

Pattern name
Problem it solves
When to use it
Structure
Why it works
Expected visual outcome
Examples

During planning, the agent should first choose these patterns based on the project, then generate the layout around them.

Components should become the implementation of the pattern—not the starting point.

The goal is for the agent to reason like a senior designer, making intentional design decisions, instead of assembling a sequence of sections.

Creative direction: Quiet premium. Palette preference: Let the agent choose. Motion preference: Story on scroll. Treat these as design constraints, not as a component template.

Concept — PD Studio: The Infinite Workspace

Instead of a traditional landing page, the entire website is one massive infinite canvas. As the user scrolls, they're not moving down a page—they're exploring a digital workspace where every area represents a different capability of PD Studio.

Core Idea

The experience starts with a single idea.

Everything else grows organically from it.

The page visually demonstrates how one idea expands into an entire company.

The Workspace

Every feature exists as its own "district."

Instead of sections stacked vertically.

Imagine walking through different rooms.

Research District

Large floating reports.

Competitor cards.

Charts.

Market analysis.

Search panels.

Animated documents.

Everything arranged like a real research table.

Design District

Beautiful landing pages pinned to a wall.

Color palettes.

Logos.

Typography.

Design systems.

UI components floating around.

Looks like an actual creative studio.

Development District

Instead of code...

Large browser previews.

Desktop mockups.

Mobile mockups.

CMS panels.

Deployment timeline.

Everything connected.

Marketing District

Instagram posts.

Facebook ads.

Email previews.

TikTok videos.

Campaign calendar.

Analytics.

Content planner.

Looks like an agency wall.

Launch District

Checklist.

Progress rings.

Revenue forecast.

Launch countdown.

Domain status.

SEO score.

Deployment animation.

Navigation Experience

The user can move anywhere.

Like Google Maps.

Zoom.

Pan.

Scroll.

Everything connected.

Floating AI

A tiny AI assistant constantly travels.

Small glowing orb.

It flies between sections.

Leaving glowing trails.

Whenever it reaches a workspace...

Animations begin.

Charts move.

Cards appear.

Numbers update.

Makes the website feel alive.

Interactive Elements

Every object is draggable.

Click reports.

Expand dashboards.

Open browser previews.

Rotate 3D product cards.

Nothing feels static.

Hidden Discoveries

As users explore...

Small easter eggs appear.

Prototype videos.

Secret product concepts.

Developer notes.

Future roadmap.

Experimental AI agents.

Encourages exploration.

Lighting

The entire page slowly changes.

Morning colors.

Afternoon.

Night.

Depending on scroll depth.

Subtle.

Almost cinematic.

CTA

Instead of a button...

The user reaches a giant empty workspace.

Center text

Your next company
starts here.

Below

A blinking cursor.

Describe your idea...

_____________________

Once they begin typing...

The interface around them starts growing.

Research cards appear.

Website previews generate.

Brand colors emerge.

The website itself demonstrates the product in real time.

Footer

There isn't one.

Instead...

The workspace simply fades into an endless grid.

One sentence remains.

Every great company started with a single idea.

Why This Stands Out
Not another scrolling SaaS page—it feels like a product demo.
The layout reinforces the product's value: ideas expanding into businesses.
Users explore instead of consume, making the experience memorable.
Every capability is represented as a physical space, creating a mental model that's easy to understand.
The CTA is integrated into the experience rather than feeling tacked on at the end.

The result is a website that feels less like marketing and more like stepping inside the future workspace that PD Studio promises to be.

Creative direction: Cinematic editorial. Palette preference: Let the agent choose. Motion preference: Story on scroll. Treat these as design constraints, not as a component template.

The page should feel like walking through a futuristic design studio rather than reading a website. Every section introduces another part of the product through interactive UI, making visitors feel they're already using PD Studio before they ever click "Open Studio."

Creative direction: Cinematic editorial. Palette preference: Let the agent choose. Motion preference: Story on scroll. Treat these as design constraints, not as a component template.

Micro Interactions
Cursor spotlight.
Buttons gently expand on hover.
Cards elevate 6–10px.
Borders glow softly.
Icons rotate slightly.
Numbers count up when visible.
Charts animate into view.
Tabs slide smoothly.

Soft page transitions.
Visual Hierarchy
Hero headline dominates the page.
Interactive Studio preview captures attention.
Feature grid explains capabilities.
Studio showcase demonstrates the workflow.
AI agents reinforce the platform's intelligence.
Analytics dashboard builds credibility.
Pricing and CTA drive conversions.
Overall Feel

The finished website should feel like:

A polished desktop application rather than a traditional marketing page.
Calm and spacious, with restrained but high-quality motion.
Focused on demonstrating the product through interactive previews instead of long blocks of text.
Premium enough that visitors immediately associate it with modern AI products such as Linear, Raycast, Vercel, and Stripe, while maintaining its own identity centered around building startups from ideas.

Creative direction: Quiet premium. Palette preference: Forest & mineral. Motion preference: Story on scroll. Treat these as design constraints, not as a component template.
